title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: Six paragraphs have been removed from the text. Choose the correct paragraph (A-G) to fill each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.title: The Quiet Revolution: Why Silence is Vital
content.text: In our modern, hyper-connected world, noise has become an ever-present background track to our lives. From the hum of traffic to the pings of our smartphones, true silence is increasingly rare. Yet, recent neurological research suggests that this lack of quiet may be doing more than just causing irritation. || (41) || [41] This growing body of evidence indicates that silence is not merely the absence of noise, but a key requirement for brain health [/41]. Scientists have found that when we are in a quiet environment, the brain doesn't just switch off; it enters a state of high-level integration. || (42) || [42] One of the most surprising discoveries in this field occurred during a study on mice [/42]. Researchers found that when the animals were exposed to two hours of silence a day, they developed new cells in the hippocampus—the region of the brain associated with memory and emotion. || (43) || [43] This finding suggests that silence could literally grow the brain [/43]. It challenges the long-held belief that only active mental stimulation, like learning a language or a new instrument, can promote neurogenesis. It appears that the brain needs 'downtime' to process information and repair itself. || (44) || [44] However, finding this degree of quiet is becoming a luxury [/44]. Acoustic ecologists warn that 'noise pollution' is now a global epidemic, affecting not only human well-being but also the behavior of wildlife. In many urban areas, there is no longer any time of day when the sound of human activity is completely absent. || (45) || [45] To combat this, some cities are experimenting with 'quiet zones' [/45]. These are areas of parks or public buildings where phones and loud conversations are banned. The goal is to provide a 'sensory refuge' for citizens overwhelmed by the constant barrage of digital and mechanical noise. || (46) || [46] But silence is not just an external condition; it is also an internal one [/46]. Even in a quiet room, our minds are often filled with an internal monologue of worries and to-do lists. Learning to cultivate 'inner silence' through practices like meditation may be as important as finding a quiet park in the city.

paragraphs.A: [42] This state is known as the 'default mode network,' and it is crucial for self-reflection and creativity [/42]. When external stimuli are removed, the brain begins to internalize information and make connections that it would otherwise ignore.
paragraphs.B: [44] It is a trend that shows no sign of slowing down [/44]. As our cities grow and our devices become more integrated into our lives, the 'acoustic commons'—the shared space of sound—is being eroded at an alarming rate.
paragraphs.C: [41] For years, noise was considered a mere 'nuisance,' a secondary environmental issue [/41]. However, we now know that chronic exposure to noise is linked to increased levels of stress hormones, heart disease, and sleep disorders.
paragraphs.D: [43] While the study was small, the implications for human health are profound [/43]. If silence can trigger cell growth, it could potentially be used as a therapeutic tool for conditions like Alzheimer’s or depression, which are characterized by the loss of neurons.
paragraphs.E: [45] The economic value of these spaces is also being recognized [/45]. Real estate developers are finding that 'acoustic comfort' is now a major selling point, with quiet apartments commanding significantly higher prices than those on busy streets.
paragraphs.F: Some researchers argue that complete silence is impossible to achieve. Even in the most advanced soundproof chambers, humans begin to hear the sound of their own heartbeat and nervous system, a sensation that many find deeply unsettling.
paragraphs.G: [46] Achieving this mental stillness, however, is often more difficult than finding physical quiet [/46]. In a world designed to capture our attention every second, the ability to simply 'be' without distraction is becoming a rare and valuable skill.

questions.explanation: C contrasta la antigua visión del ruido como 'molestia' con la evidencia actual de daños a la salud, conectando con el inicio del texto.
A define el 'default mode network' mencionado como el estado de integración que ocurre cuando el cerebro no tiene estímulos externos.
D conecta el hallazgo en ratones con las posibles implicaciones terapéuticas para enfermedades humanas como el Alzheimer.
B refuerza la idea de que el silencio es un lujo difícil de encontrar debido a la erosión de los 'acoustic commons'.
E añade la dimensión económica a la creación de 'zonas de silencio', mencionando el valor de los apartamentos silenciosos.
G conecta la idea del 'silencio interno' con la dificultad de lograr la quietud mental en un mundo lleno de distracciones.
